ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for shortbread; A003K (biscuits and cookies) used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for shortbread biscuit as a food concept; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of shortbread is one rectangular finger or round piece weighing approximately 20 g, providing about 107 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Shortbread — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080062 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with confidence 0.95. Core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for shortbread (FDC ID 172779) to supplement HPB data, which does not report fatty acid fractions separately. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient detail set to -1 where not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Shortbread GI of 64 (medium) is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for shortbread biscuit (glucose reference = 100). Glycemic load per 20 g serving calculated as: (64 × 12.8 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 8.2. Available carbohydrate per serving = (64.0 g carbohydrate − 0.0 g fibre) × 0.20 = 12.8 g. Input record listed GI 75 (high) from an unspecified embedded database; this has been superseded by the University of Sydney GI Database value of 64 as the approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (26 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; high saturated fat (17 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness independent of sodium content
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit shortbread to no more than one small piece (20 g) on rare occasions, as its high sugar and saturated fat load elevates cardiovascular risk even though sodium is negligible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (64 g/100 g), high sugar (26 g/100 g), and zero dietary fibre produce a rapid and pronounced postprandial blood glucose spike; GI 64 (medium) with high carbohydrate density yields a meaningful glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid shortbread or limit intake to a single small piece (20 g) as a very occasional treat, pairing it with a protein or fibre source to partially blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 17 g/100 g (well above the 5 g/100 g threshold) and naturally occurring trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g from butter) raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherogenesis; high sugar further promotes dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid shortbread due to its high saturated fat and trans fat content from butter, which directly elevate LDL cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Naturally occurring trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g from butter) is a hard-gate pro-inflammatory trigger; saturated fat at 17 g/100 g and sugar at 26 g/100 g further promote inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid shortbread entirely, as its butter-derived trans fat, high saturated fat, and high sugar content collectively promote systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shortbread is a traditional Scottish butter biscuit made from a simple three-ingredient base of wheat flour, butter, and sugar, typically in a ratio of 1:2:3 (sugar:butter:flour). It is baked at a low temperature to produce a characteristically pale, crumbly, and melt-in-the-mouth texture with a rich, buttery flavour and a delicate sweetness. Shortbread is associated with Scottish heritage and is widely consumed as a festive treat, particularly at Christmas and Hogmanay (Scottish New Year). It is available in several traditional shapes including rectangular fingers, round rounds, and the fan-shaped petticoat tails. Nutritionally, shortbread is energy-dense at 536 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 28 g (predominantly saturated fat at approximately 17 g from butter) and a high carbohydrate content of 64 g including 26 g of sugar. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g, meaning there is no modulation of glucose absorption. Sodium is very low. The glycemic index is 64 (medium category per University of Sydney GI Database), but the high carbohydrate density means even a small serving delivers a meaningful glycemic load. Shortbread is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, high sugar, zero fibre, and naturally occurring trans fat from butter. It is vegetarian but not vegan, and contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of shortbread?
How much fat is in shortbread?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo (cr_id F11080062, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central shortbread entry (FDC ID 172779) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. GI value updated from University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI source) superseding the unspecified embedded GI database value in the input. All primary nutrient values are from approved sources.
notes: 1. Sodium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB. This is confirmed zero per the HPB source; traditional shortbread recipes may include a small amount of salt, but the HPB record reports 0.0 g sodium. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB — shortbread contains no significant fibre-contributing ingredients. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 by HPB; these are confirmed zero per the HPB source record. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for shortbread are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 172779) was consulted for saturated fat and trans fat supplementation only. 3. Trans fat is set to 0.2 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for butter-based shortbread, reflecting naturally occurring vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid from dairy butter. 4. GI value in the input record (75, high, from 'Embedded GI Database') has been superseded by the University of Sydney GI Database value of 64 (medium) as the approved GI source per Project Veritas source policy. 5. Shortbread is a Western/Scottish food; no mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules (Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine rules do not apply). Local_names fields are left null accordingly.
